ExecutiveSummary.Finding: The application now enforces stronger JWT validation, CSRF origin checks, cookie hardening, production-safe rate limiting, owner-scoped note mutations, stricter input validation, and security headers.

Development-mode end-to-end smoke tests passed for signup, login, logout, session persistence, protected routes, notes CRUD, search, filters, dashboard counts, CSRF rejection, and cross-user access prevention.
Production build passes, but production auth routes intentionally fail closed with a configuration error until real Upstash rate-limiting credentials are present.

ThreatModel.Entry: Cookie-based JWT sessions exposed the app to token forgery, CSRF, and session fixation risks.
Notes ownership and search paths could leak or mutate another user's data if query scoping was incomplete.
Production dependencies and runtime configuration needed to fail safely without leaking internals or silently weakening rate limiting.

Vulnerability.Title: JWT verification lacked issuer, audience, and explicit algorithm enforcement
Vulnerability.Risk: Forged or malformed tokens could be accepted if they were signed with the right secret but wrong claims or algorithm.
Vulnerability.Reproduction: Before the fix, tokens with the wrong issuer, audience, or algorithm were not explicitly rejected by the verifier.
Vulnerability.Fix: JWT signing now sets subject, issued-at, expiration, issuer, and audience; verification only accepts HS256 and validates the expected claims; JWT secrets must be at least 32 characters.
Vulnerability.Verification: Unit tests now confirm rejection of malformed, forged, expired, wrong-issuer, wrong-audience, and wrong-algorithm tokens.

Vulnerability.Title: Production rate limiting could silently fall back to a local store
Vulnerability.Risk: Production brute-force protection could degrade to an insecure in-memory/file-based fallback or behave inconsistently across instances.
Vulnerability.Reproduction: Before the fix, missing or failed Upstash configuration could still allow a local fallback path.
Vulnerability.Fix: Production now fails closed with a safe configuration error when Upstash credentials are absent, while local fallback is preserved only for development/test.
Vulnerability.Verification: Production-mode smoke testing returned a generic configuration error instead of silently falling back; development-mode smoke tests passed with the local fallback.

Vulnerability.Title: Note update and delete used a fetch-then-mutate ownership pattern
Vulnerability.Risk: A time-of-check/time-of-use gap could allow stale ownership checks or leakage of note existence/ownership details.
Vulnerability.Reproduction: Before the fix, note ownership was checked in a separate read before update/delete.
Vulnerability.Fix: Update and delete now use compound `id` + `userId` filters, then safely distinguish 404 from 403 without reading note contents from another user.
Vulnerability.Verification: Unit tests cover own-note success, cross-user forbidden access, and not-found behavior.

Vulnerability.Title: State-changing routes lacked CSRF origin validation
Vulnerability.Risk: Cookie-authenticated requests could be triggered cross-site if origin checks were absent.
Vulnerability.Reproduction: Before the fix, the app did not verify the Origin header for POST/PUT/DELETE requests.
Vulnerability.Fix: All state-changing routes now reject missing or untrusted origins with a generic 403 response, while allowing same-origin and configured trusted origins.
Vulnerability.Verification: Development-mode smoke tests confirmed a cross-origin signup attempt returns 403.

Vulnerability.Title: Password policy and login timing leaks were too weak
Vulnerability.Risk: Short passwords and distinct unknown-user timing could make brute-force or enumeration easier.
Vulnerability.Reproduction: Before the fix, signup only required an 8-character password and login returned earlier for unknown users.
Vulnerability.Fix: Signup now requires 12+ characters and blocks a small weak-password list; login performs a dummy bcrypt comparison for unknown users and keeps a generic failure message.
Vulnerability.Verification: Unit tests cover password validation and login helper behavior, and the dev smoke test completed login successfully.

Vulnerability.Title: Security headers and caching controls were incomplete
Vulnerability.Risk: Missing anti-clickjacking, resource isolation, HSTS, and no-store headers could increase exposure or allow unintended caching of private data.
Vulnerability.Reproduction: Before the fix, production headers were missing several hardening directives and API responses did not explicitly opt out of caching.
Vulnerability.Fix: Production now emits X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy, X-Frame-Options, COOP, CORP, HSTS, and a stricter CSP; sensitive API responses use `Cache-Control: no-store`.
Vulnerability.Verification: Unit tests check the production header set, and the production smoke test observed the headers on real responses.

FixesApplied.Fix: Added shared security helpers for cookie naming, origin checks, safe redirects, JSON body limits, no-store responses, and security logging.
Hardened JWT issuance and verification and moved session cookies to `__Host-` in production.
Enforced CSRF origin validation on all state-changing routes and rejected oversized or malformed JSON bodies.
Scoped note queries and mutations by authenticated user, and rejected invalid UUIDs before database access.
Improved password validation, login timing behavior, and generic auth failure responses.
Added production security headers and applied no-store to auth/dashboard/notes responses and client API requests.
Added a custom TypeScript-aware security test runner and focused regression tests for the new controls.

TestsPerformed.Test: Development-mode smoke test on localhost:3000 - passed for signup, login, logout, session persistence, protected routes, notes CRUD, search, priority filter, dashboard counts, CSRF rejection, and cross-user access prevention
Production-mode smoke test on localhost:3001 - passed for protected-route redirect, security headers, and safe failure when Upstash config is absent

DependencyAudit.NpmAuditResult: 5 moderate severity vulnerabilities remain in transitive dependencies. The reported issues come from @hono/node-server via prisma dev tooling and postcss via next. Fixes would require breaking upgrades, so no unsafe upgrade was applied.
DependencyAudit.NpmOutdatedResult: Outdated packages were reported for @types/node, eslint, react, react-dom, and typescript, but the currently installed versions already match the project's wanted ranges; no risky major upgrade was performed.

EnvironmentSecurity.Note: .env files remain ignored by .gitignore and `.env.example` contains placeholders only.
No secrets were printed in this report.
EnvironmentSecurity.RequiredVariable: Required in all environments; must be at least 32 characters.
Recommended for production CSRF/origin allow-listing and redirect trust.
Optional comma-separated list for additional trusted deployment origins.
Required in production for distributed rate limiting.
Required in production for distributed rate limiting.

RemainingRisks.Risk: Production auth and note write routes will continue to fail closed until real Upstash credentials are provisioned.
Production CSP still uses `unsafe-inline` for Next.js compatibility; removing it would require a larger nonce-based integration.
npm audit still reports unresolved moderate vulnerabilities in upstream dependencies; fixing them safely requires framework/tooling upgrades.
Live database and Upstash verification were not possible in this local environment without production credentials.

DeploymentRequirements.Requirement: Provision `DATABASE_URL`, `JWT_SECRET`, `UPSTASH_REDIS_REST_URL`, and `UPSTASH_REDIS_REST_TOKEN` as Vercel Sensitive Environment Variables.
Set `APP_ORIGIN` to the canonical production origin and add any preview or alternate domains to `TRUSTED_ORIGINS`.
Use Prisma migrations for production schema changes and avoid `prisma db push` for deployment.
Use least-privilege PostgreSQL credentials with TLS enabled.
Rotate JWT and Upstash secrets separately for development, preview, and production.

ProductionReadiness.Details: The codebase now passes lint, unit security tests, and production build checks. A production deployment is still blocked until the required production secrets and Upstash credentials are supplied. Once those variables are set, the app is ready for deployment.
